summary: Mardin is where my great-grandmother Fahima Hanosh was born in 1901. It’s a place in Syria where her family lived before they emigrated to the United States. Fahima and her siblings grew up there before making the long journey to join their family in America around 1920.

Syria is the country where my great-grandmother Fahima Hanosh and her family originally lived. They were from Mardin, Syria, and it was there that Fahima’s parents, Abdul Amseh and Saydoon Hamany, married around 1885. The family lived there until the early 20th century before emigrating to the United States, with some members passing through Canada first.

The United States became home to my great-grandmother Fahima Hanosh and her family starting around 1914 to 1920. Her father and eldest brothers established themselves here first, working as peddlers and grocers in Michigan. Fahima joined them in 1920, married twice to fellow Syrian immigrants, and raised her family here, embracing assimilation and education.

Joseph Anton was my great-grandmother Fahima Hanosh’s first husband, whom she married in 1922. He was a fellow Syrian immigrant and a shoemaker. They had two children together, Said and Alice, but the marriage was unhappy and ended in divorce by 1927, partly due to his drinking during the Prohibition era.

John, or Jack Oirathy, was my great-grandmother Fahima Hanosh’s second husband. He immigrated in 1916 via Canada and worked as a tool and dye maker. They married soon after her divorce from Joseph Anton and had two children together, Rose Marie and my grandmother Jamilla, also known as Jimmy.

Albert Hanosh was one of Fahima’s older brothers who emigrated before her and helped establish the family in the United States. He worked as a grocer, a trade his father secured for him after arriving from Syria. Albert was part of the family’s early efforts to settle in Michigan by 1921.

Mary Hanosh was Fahima’s older sister who emigrated with her in the summer of 1920. She was part of the siblings who joined their father and brothers in the United States to reunite the family. Mary’s journey was part of the family’s collective migration from Syria to Michigan.

Charlie Hanosh, also known as Constantin, was one of Fahima’s older brothers who came to the U.S. before her. Like Albert, he worked as a grocer after their father secured the trade for him. Charlie was part of the family’s foundation in Michigan by the early 1920s.

I am the great-granddaughter of Fahima Hanosh, who was born in Mardin, Syria in 1901. She emigrated to the United States in 1920 with her siblings to join their father and brothers who had settled in Michigan. Fahima married twice, first to Joseph Anton and then to John Oirathy, and raised a family while working as a housekeeper and dressmaker. Her culture and values of assimilation and education have been passed down through the generations.

Sophia Hanosh was one of Fahima’s younger siblings born in Syria. While not much is detailed about her in the family story, she was part of the seven children born to Abdul Amseh and Saydoon Hamany before the family emigrated to the United States.

Jamilia Hanosh, also known as Jimmy, is my grandmother and the daughter of Fahima Hanosh and John Oirathy. She was born and raised in Michigan, part of a family that valued assimilation and education. Jimmy married in 1950 and continued the family legacy into the next generation.

Shaukry, also called Frank, was one of Fahima’s younger brothers born in Syria. Like his siblings, he was part of the Hanosh family that eventually emigrated to the United States. Though less is mentioned about him, he was part of the family’s roots in Syria.

Abdul Amseh Hanosh was my great-great-grandfather who married Saydoon Hamany in Syria around 1885. He immigrated to the United States between 1914 and 1916, initially working as a peddler before becoming a grocer. He helped secure trades for his sons Albert and Charlie, establishing the family’s livelihood in Michigan.

Saydoon, also known as Sadie, was my great-great-grandmother married to Abdul Amseh Hanosh in Syria around 1885. Her parents introduced her to Amseh, and they married after getting to know each other as children. She was the mother of seven children, including Fahima, and part of the family that emigrated to the U.S.

The Detroit Free Press was the newspaper that published announcements about divorces during the Prohibition era. It was through this paper that I learned about Fahima Hanosh’s divorce from Joseph Anton, where she publicly accused him of making his own moonshine. This detail gives insight into the difficulties Fahima faced during that time.

Prohibition was a significant event during the 1920s that affected my great-grandmother Fahima Hanosh’s life. Her first husband, Joseph Anton, was accused of making his own moonshine, which was a serious offense then. This period was marked by strict alcohol laws and contributed to the difficulties in their marriage, ending in divorce in 1927.

Canada was a transit point for some members of my great-grandmother’s family on their way to the United States. Both Fahima’s father, Abdul Amseh Hanosh, and her second husband, John Oirathy, immigrated via Canada before settling in Michigan. This route was common for Syrian immigrants during the early 20th century.

Tabbouleh is a traditional dish that connects me to my great-grandmother Fahima Hanosh’s Syrian heritage. Even today, my child eats tabbouleh with a baby spoon, and we say 'saha' at appropriate times, keeping the culture alive in our family. It’s a small but meaningful way the Hanosh family’s traditions continue through generations.
